rizzolatti: Giacomo Rizzolatti (Parma, 1990s). Discovered mirror neurons in macaque area F5 (premotor cortex), firing both during action and during observation of the same action. Foundation for theory of mind / imitation accounts.
giacomo rizzolatti: Italian neurophysiologist. Discovered mirror neurons in macaque ventral premotor area F5 (1990s) with Fadiga, Gallese & Fogassi. Now linked to action understanding, imitation, language evolution.
mirror neurons: Neurons (first found in macaque F5) that fire both when an animal performs an action and when it sees another perform it. Discovered by Rizzolatti group; cited in theories of imitation, empathy, autism.
premotor cortex: Lateral frontal motor area anterior to M1 (Brodmann 6). Plans externally-guided movements (visual / auditory cues). Site of macaque mirror neurons. Damage → apraxia.
supplementary motor area: Medial Brodmann 6 (SMA). Plans internally-generated, sequenced and bimanual movements. Active before voluntary action (Bereitschaftspotential).
sma: Supplementary Motor Area (medial Brodmann 6). Plans internally-generated sequenced movement; readiness potential originates here.
primary motor cortex: M1, Brodmann 4, precentral gyrus. Outputs corticospinal tract. Contains Betz cells. Homunculus-organised body map; population coding, not single-cell.
m1: Primary motor cortex (Brodmann 4, precentral gyrus). Sends 60% of corticospinal fibers. Houses giant Betz pyramidal cells.
posterior parietal cortex: Multisensory association cortex behind S1 (Brodmann 5/7). Integrates body schema + visual/auditory space to plan reaching/grasping. Damage → apraxia, contralateral neglect.
contralateral neglect: Failure to attend to / report stimuli on the side opposite a brain lesion (usu. right parietal). Often egocentric (body-centred). Patient ignores left side of plate, draws only right half of clock.
apraxia: Loss of skilled, learned movement despite intact strength, sensation and comprehension. Typically left parietal damage. Ideomotor (can't pantomime) vs. ideational (can't sequence) subtypes.
astereognosia: Inability to recognise objects by touch alone with eyes closed. Lesion of secondary somatosensory cortex (S2) / posterior parietal.
betz cells: Giant pyramidal neurons in layer V of M1. Source of about 10% of corticospinal axons — the largest neurons in the cortex.
corticospinal tract: Main descending motor pathway from cortex to spinal cord. Lateral (decussates at medullary pyramids, controls distal limbs) and ventral (uncrossed, axial muscles) divisions.
dorsolateral corticospinal tract: Lateral corticospinal tract. Decussates at the pyramidal decussation in the medulla. Controls distal limb muscles (fingers, fine movement). Synapses directly on motor neurons.
dorsolateral corticorubrospinal tract: Crosses in midbrain via the red nucleus, then descends laterally. Controls distal limbs in parallel to corticospinal — backup/secondary route.
ventromedial corticospinal tract: Uncrossed; controls bilateral axial/proximal muscles for posture and whole-body movement.
decussation: Crossing of fibers from one side of the CNS to the other. Pyramidal decussation in the medulla is the corticospinal crossing.
pyramidal decussation: Crossing of the lateral corticospinal tract at the medulla–spinal cord junction. Why right cortex controls left distal limb.
basal ganglia: Subcortical nuclei: caudate, putamen, globus pallidus (internal GPi + external GPe), subthalamic nucleus, substantia nigra. Action selection and habit learning. Damage → Parkinson, Huntington, hemiballismus.
striatum: Input nuclei of the basal ganglia: caudate + putamen. Receives cortical glutamate and SNc dopamine. Damaged in Huntington's chorea.
caudate: C-shaped striatal nucleus running with the lateral ventricle. Cognitive / associative loop of basal ganglia. Atrophy in Huntington.
putamen: Lateral striatal nucleus. Motor loop input of basal ganglia. Receives heavy SNc dopamine.
globus pallidus: Pallidum: GPe + GPi. GPi is the major basal-ganglia output to thalamus, tonically inhibitory; direct pathway suppresses GPi → disinhibits thalamus → movement.
gpi: Globus pallidus internal segment. Tonic GABAergic output of basal ganglia; inhibits motor thalamus. Direct pathway turns it OFF (movement); indirect turns it ON (no movement).
gpe: Globus pallidus external segment. Way-station in the indirect pathway; inhibits the subthalamic nucleus.
subthalamic nucleus: Glutamatergic basal-ganglia node in the indirect pathway. Drives GPi (brake). Damage → hemiballismus. Target of deep-brain stimulation in Parkinson.
substantia nigra: Midbrain nucleus. SNc (compacta) is dopaminergic, projects to striatum (nigrostriatal pathway). Degeneration → Parkinson disease. SNr (reticulata) outputs like GPi.
snc: Substantia nigra pars compacta. Dopamine to striatum. Degenerates in Parkinson disease (>60% loss before symptoms).
direct pathway: 'Go' basal-ganglia loop: cortex → striatum → GPi (inhibit) → thalamus disinhibition → cortex. Facilitates movement. Boosted by D1 dopamine.
indirect pathway: 'No-go' basal-ganglia loop: cortex → striatum → GPe → STN → GPi → thalamus inhibition. Suppresses movement. Boosted by D2 dopamine.
hyperdirect pathway: Cortex → subthalamic nucleus → GPi, bypassing striatum. Provides a fast, broad motor brake; relevant to stopping signals and OCD/impulsivity.
hemiballismus: Wild flinging movements of one side, classically from contralateral subthalamic-nucleus lesion (stroke). Disinhibits motor thalamus.
huntington's disease: Autosomal-dominant CAG trinucleotide expansion (HTT gene, chr 4). Chorea, cognitive decline, psychiatric symptoms. Caudate atrophy. Onset ~30-50.
parkinson's disease: Degeneration of dopaminergic SNc neurons. Triad: resting tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia. Lewy bodies (α-synuclein). Treat L-DOPA, DBS of STN/GPi.
dystonia: Sustained involuntary muscle contractions causing twisting / abnormal postures. Often basal-ganglia origin.
wilson's disease: Autosomal-recessive copper accumulation (ATP7B). Basal-ganglia deposits → tremor, dystonia, psychiatric features. Kayser–Fleischer rings.
cerebellum: 'Little brain' behind the brainstem. 10% of brain mass, >half its neurons. Coordinates timing, posture, motor learning, and increasingly cognition.
cerebrocerebellum: Lateral cerebellar hemispheres. Receives heavy cortical input via pontine nuclei. Plans/times skilled, learned movement; cognitive functions.
spinocerebellum: Vermis + paravermis. Sensorimotor integration; ongoing movement correction.
vestibulocerebellum: Flocculonodular lobe. Balance, posture, vestibular-ocular reflex.
purkinje cells: Sole output neurons of the cerebellar cortex. GABAergic, inhibitory, project to deep cerebellar nuclei. Receive mossy (via granule cells) and climbing fiber input.
mossy fibers: Main cerebellar input from pontine nuclei and spinal cord. Synapse on granule cells, which excite Purkinje cells via parallel fibers. Many-to-one signal; carries context/sensory info for motor learning.
climbing fibers: Cerebellar input from the inferior olivary nucleus. Each Purkinje cell gets exactly one climbing fiber → powerful complex spikes. Carry error signals that drive cerebellar motor learning (LTD at parallel-fiber synapses).
granule cells: Tiny cerebellar interneurons (~half of all CNS neurons). Receive mossy fibers, send parallel fibers to Purkinje cells.
inferior olive: Brainstem nucleus that gives rise to climbing fibers. Encodes motor error / timing signals for cerebellar learning.
comparator model: Cerebellum compares intended movement (efference copy) with actual feedback to generate corrective output. Mismatch → online correction + long-term motor learning.
ballistic movements: Fast, pre-programmed movements (e.g. throwing a dart). Too quick for sensory feedback; cerebellum supplies the program.
ataxia: Loss of coordinated movement. Cerebellar damage → intention tremor, dysmetria, dysdiadochokinesia, wide-based gait, scanning speech.
hypotonia: Reduced muscle tone. Classic cerebellar lesion sign.
antagonistic muscles: Muscle pair acting at the same joint in opposite directions (flexor + extensor). Reciprocal innervation.
extrafusal muscle: Standard skeletal muscle fibers innervated by α-motor neurons; generate force.
intrafusal muscle: Specialised fibers inside muscle spindles. Innervated by γ-motor neurons; set spindle sensitivity to stretch.
muscle spindle: Stretch receptor inside skeletal muscle. Ia afferents signal length and velocity. Drives the stretch reflex.
golgi tendon organ: Tension receptor at the muscle-tendon junction (Ib afferent). Triggers inverse stretch reflex to protect muscle from overload.
stretch reflex: Monosynaptic Ia → α-motor neuron loop. Tap patellar tendon → quadriceps contracts. Tests spinal-cord integrity at the dermatome.
withdrawal reflex: Polysynaptic spinal reflex that pulls a limb away from a noxious stimulus; uses crossed extension on the other side for balance.
central pattern generator: Spinal-cord circuit that generates rhythmic motor output (walking, swimming) without higher input. Demonstrated in spinal cats on treadmills.
alpha motor neuron: Spinal motor neuron that innervates extrafusal muscle fibers; the final common pathway. Cell body in ventral horn.
gamma motor neuron: Spinal motor neuron innervating intrafusal fibers of muscle spindles. Maintains spindle sensitivity during muscle shortening.
renshaw cell: Inhibitory spinal interneuron. Receives α-motor neuron collateral; feeds back inhibition (recurrent collateral inhibition) to limit motor neuron firing.
frontal eye field: Brodmann 8. Controls voluntary saccadic eye movements. Damage → contralateral gaze palsy.
broca's area: Left inferior frontal gyrus (Brodmann 44/45). Speech production. Damage → non-fluent / expressive aphasia (Broca's aphasia).
wernicke's area: Left posterior superior temporal gyrus (Brodmann 22). Speech comprehension. Damage → fluent but meaningless speech, poor comprehension (Wernicke's aphasia).
conduction aphasia: Lesion of the arcuate fasciculus disconnecting Broca and Wernicke. Fluent speech, good comprehension, but cannot repeat.
arcuate fasciculus: White-matter tract connecting Wernicke's to Broca's area. Damage → conduction aphasia.
hippocampus: Medial temporal-lobe structure essential for forming new declarative memories. Bilateral damage → anterograde amnesia (patient H.M.).
Henry Molaison. Bilateral medial-temporal lobectomy (1953) for epilepsy. Severe anterograde amnesia, preserved IQ + procedural learning. Foundational case in memory research.
amygdala: Almond-shaped temporal-lobe nucleus. Fear conditioning, emotional salience, social signals. Kluver-Bucy after bilateral lesion.
kluver-bucy syndrome: After bilateral temporal-lobe / amygdala damage: hyperorality, hypersexuality, placidity, visual agnosia, hypermetamorphosis.
hypothalamus: Master regulator of homeostasis + endocrine + autonomic output. Pea-sized, just below thalamus. Houses SCN, PVN, VMH, LH, etc.
scn: Suprachiasmatic nucleus. Master circadian pacemaker in hypothalamus, entrained by retinal light input (ipRGCs → SCN).
vmh: Ventromedial hypothalamus. 'Satiety center.' Lesion → hyperphagia + obesity (VMH syndrome).
lh: Lateral hypothalamus. 'Feeding center' / arousal. Lesion → aphagia, weight loss, somnolence.
pvn: Paraventricular nucleus of hypothalamus. Source of CRH (HPA axis), oxytocin, vasopressin to posterior pituitary.
thalamus: Sensory and motor relay between subcortex and cortex. Major nuclei: LGN (vision), MGN (audition), VPL/VPM (somatosens.), VL (motor).
lgn: Lateral geniculate nucleus of thalamus. Visual relay from retinal ganglion cells to V1. Six layers; 1-2 magnocellular, 3-6 parvocellular.
mgn: Medial geniculate nucleus of thalamus. Auditory relay to primary auditory cortex.
v1: Primary visual cortex (Brodmann 17, striate cortex, occipital pole). First cortical stage of vision; retinotopic, orientation columns.
ventral stream: 'What' visual pathway: V1 → temporal lobe. Object/face identification. Damage → visual agnosia, prosopagnosia.
dorsal stream: 'Where/how' visual pathway: V1 → parietal lobe. Spatial vision, action guidance. Damage → optic ataxia, simultanagnosia.
prosopagnosia: Face blindness from damage to the fusiform face area (right occipitotemporal). Patient cannot recognise familiar faces; objects intact.
blindsight: After V1 damage, patient denies seeing yet performs above chance on visual tasks. Mediated by extrageniculate (SC → pulvinar) pathway.
balint's syndrome: Bilateral posterior parietal damage: simultanagnosia (one object at a time), optic ataxia, oculomotor apraxia.
action potential: All-or-none ~1 ms electrical spike. Voltage-gated Na+ in (depolarisation) then K+ out (repolarisation) at the axon hillock. Threshold ~−55 mV from resting −70.
resting potential: ≈ −70 mV inside vs. outside. Set by K+ leak channels + Na/K-ATPase + impermeable anions. Predicted by the Goldman equation.
depolarisation: Membrane potential becomes less negative. Excitatory; if past threshold, fires an action potential.
hyperpolarisation: Membrane potential becomes more negative than rest. Inhibitory; reduces likelihood of firing.
epsp: Excitatory postsynaptic potential. Graded depolarisation from glutamate at AMPA/NMDA, summed at the axon hillock.
ipsp: Inhibitory postsynaptic potential. Graded hyperpolarisation from GABA-A (Cl− in) or GABA-B (K+ out).
myelin: Lipid insulation around axons. CNS oligodendrocytes (multi-axon), PNS Schwann cells (one axon each). Enables saltatory conduction.
saltatory conduction: Action potential 'jumps' between Nodes of Ranvier on myelinated axons — fast, energy-efficient.
nodes of ranvier: Unmyelinated gaps along myelinated axons, packed with voltage-gated Na+. Sites of action-potential regeneration.
oligodendrocyte: CNS glial cell that myelinates multiple axons. Damaged in multiple sclerosis.
schwann cell: PNS glial cell. Each one myelinates a single internode of one axon. Supports peripheral nerve regeneration.
astrocyte: Star-shaped CNS glia. K+ buffering, neurotransmitter recycling, blood-brain barrier, scar formation after injury.
microglia: CNS resident immune cells. Phagocytose debris, prune synapses, respond to injury.
neurotransmitter: Chemical messenger released from presynaptic vesicles into the synaptic cleft. Binds postsynaptic receptors. Classified as small-molecule (ACh, monoamines, amino acids) or peptide.
acetylcholine: First-discovered NT (Otto Loewi, 1921). NMJ, autonomic ganglia, basal forebrain → cortex (memory). Loss → Alzheimer. Receptors: nicotinic (ionotropic) + muscarinic (metabotropic).
dopamine: Catecholamine NT. Pathways: nigrostriatal (motor), mesolimbic (reward), mesocortical (cognition), tuberoinfundibular (prolactin). Hypoactive → Parkinson; hyperactive (mesolimbic) → psychosis.
serotonin: 5-HT. Raphe nuclei → cortex. Mood, sleep, appetite, aggression. SSRIs treat depression/OCD by blocking reuptake.
norepinephrine: Catecholamine NT. Locus coeruleus → cortex. Arousal, attention, vigilance, sympathetic NS. Implicated in depression, PTSD.
gaba: Main inhibitory NT in adult CNS. GABA-A (Cl− ionotropic, fast) + GABA-B (K+ metabotropic, slow). Benzodiazepines + alcohol potentiate GABA-A.
glutamate: Main excitatory NT in CNS. Ionotropic AMPA/NMDA/kainate + metabotropic mGluR. NMDA gates Ca2+ for LTP.
ltp: Long-term potentiation. Lasting strengthening of a synapse after high-frequency stimulation. Hippocampal NMDA-dependent form is the cellular model of learning (Bliss & Lømo 1973).
ltd: Long-term depression. Lasting weakening of a synapse. Cerebellar parallel-fiber → Purkinje LTD is driven by climbing-fiber error signals → motor learning.
nmda receptor: Glutamate receptor permeable to Ca2+. Mg2+ block lifts only when both glutamate-bound AND depolarised — a coincidence detector. Drives LTP. Ketamine, PCP, alcohol block it.
ampa receptor: Glutamate receptor mediating fast Na+ EPSPs. Inserted into the membrane during LTP — the mechanism of synaptic strengthening.
hebbian learning: 'Cells that fire together, wire together.' Donald Hebb (1949). Proposes coincident pre + post activity strengthens a synapse. LTP is its cellular substrate.
donald hebb: Canadian psychologist. The Organization of Behavior (1949) — cell assemblies + Hebbian rule, enriched-environment effects.
synapse: Specialised contact site between neurons. Chemical (most) or electrical (gap junctions). Pre- and postsynaptic membranes + cleft.
synaptic cleft: ~20-40 nm gap at a chemical synapse. NT diffuses across to bind postsynaptic receptors.
vesicle: Membrane-bound sphere holding ~thousands of NT molecules in the presynaptic terminal. Releases content into cleft upon Ca2+ influx.
exocytosis: Vesicle fusion with the presynaptic membrane to release NT. Triggered by Ca2+ entry via voltage-gated channels.
reuptake: Active reabsorption of NT into the presynaptic terminal by transporter proteins. SSRI / SNRI / cocaine block monoamine reuptake.
g-protein coupled receptor: Metabotropic 7-TM receptor that activates intracellular G-protein → second messengers. Slower, longer-lasting than ionotropic.
ionotropic receptor: Ligand-gated ion channel. Fast, milliseconds. e.g. nicotinic ACh, GABA-A, NMDA/AMPA/kainate.
metabotropic receptor: GPCR. NT triggers a second-messenger cascade — slower, modulatory. e.g. muscarinic ACh, all dopamine receptors, mGluR.
agonist: Drug that binds and activates a receptor, mimicking the endogenous ligand.
antagonist: Drug that binds a receptor without activating it, blocking the endogenous ligand.
blood-brain barrier: Tight endothelial junctions + astrocytic end-feet that limit which molecules cross from blood into CNS. Lipid-soluble compounds cross easily; large polar molecules don't.
csf: Cerebrospinal fluid. Made by choroid plexus, circulates through ventricles + subarachnoid space, drains via arachnoid villi. Buoyancy + waste removal.
ventricles: Four CSF-filled cavities: paired lateral, third (midline diencephalon), fourth (between pons/medulla + cerebellum). Enlarged in hydrocephalus + schizophrenia.
meninges: Three CNS coverings: dura, arachnoid, pia. Subarachnoid space holds CSF.
phineas gage: 1848 Vermont railroad foreman. Tamping iron blew through left frontal lobe; lived. Personality changed: 'no longer Gage.' Foundational case for prefrontal–personality link.
wilder penfield: Canadian neurosurgeon (Montreal). Mapped motor + sensory homunculus by cortical stimulation during awake epilepsy surgery (1930s-50s).
homunculus: Distorted body map on cortical surface. Motor (M1) + sensory (S1) homunculi, with face + hands disproportionately large. Penfield's Montreal procedure.
olds and milner: 1954, McGill. Discovered intracranial self-stimulation in rats — electrodes in septum / MFB / NAcc; founding evidence for brain reward system.
medial forebrain bundle: Major reward / motivation tract connecting VTA → NAcc / prefrontal cortex. Highest self-stimulation rates.
nucleus accumbens: Ventral striatum. Receives VTA dopamine. Core reward/incentive-salience structure. Hyperactive in addiction.
vta: Ventral tegmental area. Midbrain dopamine source of the mesolimbic + mesocortical pathways. Activated by all addictive drugs.
mesolimbic pathway: VTA → nucleus accumbens (+ amygdala, hippocampus). Reward, motivation, addiction. Overactive in positive symptoms of schizophrenia.
mesocortical pathway: VTA → prefrontal cortex. Cognition, motivation, working memory. Underactive in negative symptoms of schizophrenia.
nigrostriatal pathway: Substantia nigra (SNc) → dorsal striatum. Motor control. Degenerates in Parkinson.
tuberoinfundibular pathway: Hypothalamus → median eminence. Inhibits prolactin. Antipsychotic D2 block → hyperprolactinemia → galactorrhoea, amenorrhoea.
wanting: Berridge's incentive-salience component of reward. Mesolimbic dopamine = 'wanting' (motivation), NOT pleasure itself.
liking: Berridge's hedonic component of reward. Opioid + GABA hotspots in nucleus accumbens / ventral pallidum. Distinct from dopaminergic 'wanting'.
incentive salience: Berridge's theory: dopamine attaches motivational pull to reward cues. Sensitised in addiction (wanting >> liking).
tolerance: Need increasing dose for same effect. Pharmacodynamic (receptor down-regulation) or pharmacokinetic (faster metabolism).
withdrawal: Opposite-direction symptoms when a chronic drug is stopped. Reflects neuroadaptation. Severity scales with drug half-life.
korsakoff's syndrome: Thiamine (B1) deficiency, classically chronic alcoholism. Anterograde amnesia + confabulation + apathy. Diencephalic damage (mammillary bodies, dorsomedial thalamus).
wernicke's encephalopathy: Acute thiamine deficiency. Triad: confusion + ataxia + ophthalmoplegia (CN VI nystagmus). Untreated → Korsakoff. IV thiamine reverses.
alzheimer's disease: Commonest dementia. Amyloid-β plaques + tau tangles. Cholinergic loss (nucleus basalis of Meynert). Early hippocampal atrophy → anterograde amnesia.
fmri: Functional MRI. Measures BOLD (blood-oxygen-level-dependent) signal as proxy for neural activity. Good spatial (~mm), poor temporal (~s).
pet: Positron Emission Tomography. Injected radioisotope tracer (e.g. FDG for metabolism, raclopride for D2). Quantitative, but radiation + low temporal resolution.
eeg: Electroencephalography. Scalp electrodes recording summed cortical postsynaptic potentials. Excellent temporal (ms), poor spatial. Detects rhythms (delta-theta-alpha-beta-gamma).
erp: Event-related potential. EEG averaged over many trials, time-locked to a stimulus. P300 = attention/novelty; N400 = semantic violation; MMN = pre-attentive change.
tms: Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation. Coil delivers magnetic pulse, inducing focal cortical current. Used as a 'virtual lesion' or as rTMS treatment for depression.
tdcs: Transcranial Direct Current Stimulation. Weak (~1-2 mA) scalp current biases cortical excitability. Cheap; effects modest and contested.
optogenetics: Light-activated opsins (channelrhodopsin, halorhodopsin) expressed in target neurons via viral vector. Allows ms-scale on/off control of defined cell types.
single-unit recording: Microelectrode records action potentials from one neuron. Hubel & Wiesel cat V1; orientation tuning.
hubel and wiesel: 1959-onward. Recorded cat V1; discovered simple, complex, hypercomplex cells; orientation columns; critical period via monocular deprivation. Nobel 1981.
critical period: Time window of heightened plasticity when experience shapes circuitry (e.g. ocular dominance during early monocular deprivation). Closes thereafter.
neuroplasticity: Brain's ability to reorganise. Includes synaptic strength changes (LTP/LTD), dendritic remodelling, adult neurogenesis (DG, SVZ).
neurogenesis: Birth of new neurons. Persists in adult dentate gyrus + subventricular zone. Promoted by exercise + enriched environment; suppressed by stress + alcohol.
stem cells: Undifferentiated cells that self-renew and produce specialised daughters. Embryonic (totipotent → pluripotent) and adult/somatic (multipotent). Source of adult neurogenesis.
enriched environment: Cage with toys, social peers, complex layout. Increases neurogenesis, dendritic branching, cortical thickness vs. isolated controls.
stress: Sustained perceived demand. HPA axis: CRH → ACTH → cortisol. Chronic cortisol shrinks hippocampus, enlarges amygdala, raises CV risk.
hpa axis: Hypothalamus (PVN CRH) → anterior pituitary (ACTH) → adrenal cortex (cortisol). Negative feedback at hippocampus + hypothalamus. Dysregulated in depression, PTSD.
cortisol: Glucocorticoid from adrenal cortex. Mobilises glucose, suppresses immune/reproductive function. Chronic elevation → hippocampal atrophy.
sympathetic nervous system: Fight-or-flight branch of the ANS. T1–L2 outflow → ganglia → noradrenergic post-ganglionic to organs; adrenal medulla releases EPI/NE.
parasympathetic nervous system: Rest-and-digest branch. Cranial (III, VII, IX, X) + sacral (S2-4). Long preganglionic ACh → short postganglionic ACh. Vagus mediates most of it.
vagus nerve: Cranial nerve X. ~75% of parasympathetic output. Heart rate, GI, larynx. Vagal tone marker of regulation; VNS used in refractory epilepsy + depression.
rem sleep: Rapid-eye-movement / paradoxical sleep. EEG resembles wake; muscle atonia; vivid dreams. Driven by pontine PGO waves. ~20-25% of sleep, more in second half of night.
non-rem sleep: Stages N1-N3. N3 = slow-wave (delta) deep sleep, restorative, dominant in first half of night. Sleepwalking, night terrors, bedwetting occur in N3.
circadian rhythm: ~24 h biological cycle. Driven by SCN, entrained by retinal light, output via melatonin from pineal gland.
melatonin: Pineal-gland hormone secreted in darkness. Signals night to the body, advances sleep onset, weakly soporific.
narcolepsy: Excessive daytime sleepiness + sudden REM intrusions. Cataplexy (emotion-triggered atonia), sleep paralysis, hypnagogic hallucinations. Loss of hypothalamic orexin neurons.
orexin: Aka hypocretin. Lateral-hypothalamus peptide that stabilises wakefulness. Loss → narcolepsy with cataplexy.
sleep paralysis: Brief inability to move at sleep-wake transition; REM atonia intruding into wake. Can be normal or part of narcolepsy.
sleep apnea: Repeated breathing pauses in sleep. Obstructive (collapse) or central. Fragmented sleep, daytime hypoxia, hypertension risk.
schizophrenia: Chronic psychotic disorder. Positive (hallucinations, delusions), negative (avolition, flat affect), cognitive symptoms. Dopamine hypothesis: mesolimbic hyperactivity + mesocortical hypoactivity. Ventricular enlargement.
dopamine hypothesis: Schizophrenia model: positive symptoms = mesolimbic D2 hyperactivity (why antipsychotics block D2); negative/cognitive = mesocortical D1 hypoactivity.
depression: Major Depressive Disorder. Low mood + anhedonia + neurovegetative changes ≥2 weeks. Monoamine hypothesis (low 5-HT/NE), HPA hyperactivity, hippocampal atrophy.
monoamine hypothesis: Depression theory: deficiency of serotonin and/or NE at the synapse. Basis for SSRIs/SNRIs/TCAs/MAOIs.
neuroleptic: Antipsychotic. Typical (haloperidol) = pure D2 block; effective for positive symptoms, causes EPS. Atypical (clozapine, risperidone) = D2 + 5-HT2A block; lower EPS, broader action.
tardive dyskinesia: Late-onset involuntary movements (orofacial chorea, etc.) after long-term typical antipsychotic. D2 supersensitivity. Often irreversible.
extrapyramidal symptoms: Motor side effects of D2 antagonism in nigrostriatal pathway: acute dystonia, parkinsonism, akathisia, tardive dyskinesia.
lithium: Mood stabiliser for bipolar disorder. Narrow therapeutic window (0.6-1.2 mEq/L). Reduces phosphoinositide signalling. Renal + thyroid + teratogenic risks.
benzodiazepines: GABA-A positive modulators (bind α-subunit). Anxiolytic, hypnotic, anticonvulsant, muscle relaxant. Tolerance, dependence, withdrawal seizures.
ssri: Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitor (fluoxetine, sertraline). First-line for MDD, anxiety, OCD. 2-4 weeks onset; sexual side effects.
snri: Serotonin–Norepinephrine Reuptake Inhibitor (venlafaxine, duloxetine). MDD, anxiety, neuropathic pain.
tricyclic: Older antidepressant (imipramine, amitriptyline). Block 5-HT + NE reuptake plus muscarinic / histaminic / α-adrenergic — heavy side-effect load; cardiotoxic in overdose.
maoi: Monoamine Oxidase Inhibitor. Blocks NE/5-HT/DA breakdown. Effective but dietary tyramine restriction (hypertensive crisis).
ect: Electroconvulsive therapy. Brief electrical seizure under anaesthesia. Most effective treatment for severe / treatment-resistant depression. Transient retrograde amnesia.
cbt: Cognitive Behavioural Therapy (Beck). Identifies + reshapes maladaptive thoughts and behaviours. Strong evidence base for depression, anxiety, OCD, PTSD.
operant conditioning: Skinner. Behaviour is shaped by its consequences — reinforcement (positive/negative) increases it, punishment decreases it.
classical conditioning: Pavlov. A neutral stimulus (CS) paired with a US comes to elicit the response (CR) on its own. Acquisition, extinction, spontaneous recovery, generalisation.
pavlov: Russian physiologist (1904 Nobel for digestion). Discovered classical conditioning in dogs — salivation to a bell paired with food.
b.f. skinner: Radical behaviourist. Operant conditioning, the Skinner box, reinforcement schedules (FR, VR, FI, VI), 'Walden Two', programmed instruction.
skinner box: Operant chamber. Lever press → food/shock; measures rate of responding. Foundation of operant analysis.
shaping: Operant procedure: reinforce successive approximations toward a target behaviour. Used to teach complex novel responses.
reinforcement schedule: Rules for delivering reinforcers. Ratios (FR, VR) make response rate; intervals (FI, VI) make stable patterns. VR most resistant to extinction (gambling).
extinction: Progressive weakening of a CR or operant response when CS/reinforcer is withheld. Not unlearning — new inhibitory learning (Bouton).
spontaneous recovery: Reappearance of an extinguished CR after a delay without further training. Evidence that extinction is inhibitory, not erasure.
habituation: Decreased response to a repeated, harmless stimulus. Simplest form of learning. Stimulus-specific (vs. fatigue).
sensitisation: Heightened response to all stimuli after a strong/noxious one. Generalised; opposite of habituation. Aplysia gill-withdrawal model (Kandel).
eric kandel: Studied Aplysia gill-withdrawal reflex; nailed cellular mechanisms of habituation, sensitisation, classical conditioning (cAMP/CREB). 2000 Nobel.
aplysia: Sea slug with ~20,000 large neurons. Kandel's model organism for cellular learning — mapped synaptic changes in gill-withdrawal habituation/sensitisation.
bandura: Albert Bandura. Social-cognitive theory. Bobo doll (observational learning), self-efficacy, reciprocal determinism, agentic perspective.
bobo doll: Bandura's classic 1961-63 experiments. Children imitate aggressive adult models. Evidence for observational/vicarious learning.
self-efficacy: Bandura. Belief in one's capacity to perform actions needed to attain a goal. Predicts effort + persistence. Built from mastery, modelling, persuasion, arousal.
tolman: Edward Tolman. Cognitive maps, latent learning, purposive behaviourism. Showed rats build internal maps even without reinforcement.
cognitive map: Internal mental representation of spatial layout. Tolman's rats; O'Keefe & Moser hippocampal place + grid cells (2014 Nobel).
place cells: Hippocampal neurons that fire in specific spatial locations (O'Keefe 1971). Form a cognitive map.
grid cells: Entorhinal cortex neurons that fire on a triangular lattice across an environment (Moser & Moser). Provide metric coordinates of space.
engram: Physical/biological trace of memory in the brain. Lashley searched in vain; Tonegawa group has now optogenetically tagged + reactivated mouse engrams.
karl lashley: Engram search. Trained rats then lesioned cortex; conclusions: mass action + equipotentiality — memory is distributed, not stored in one place.
milner: Brenda Milner. Studied H.M., demonstrated dissociation between intact procedural and impaired declarative memory.
declarative memory: Conscious 'knowing that.' Episodic + semantic. Medial temporal lobe / hippocampus dependent.
procedural memory: Non-declarative 'knowing how.' Motor + cognitive skills, classical conditioning, priming. Spared in amnesia (H.M. learned mirror tracing).
episodic memory: Memory for specific events tied to time + place (Tulving). 'I had eggs yesterday.' Hippocampus + medial temporal.
semantic memory: Memory for facts, meanings, concepts (Tulving). 'Paris is the capital of France.' Temporal-lobe networks.
working memory: Baddeley & Hitch. Temporary store + manipulator. Phonological loop, visuospatial sketchpad, episodic buffer, central executive. ~7 ± 2 items / ~30 s.
central executive: Baddeley's attentional supervisor of working memory. Allocates resources, coordinates phonological loop + sketchpad + episodic buffer. Dorsolateral PFC substrate.
phonological loop: Verbal slave system in Baddeley's WM: phonological store (~2 s) + articulatory rehearsal. Explains word-length + phonological-similarity effects.
visuospatial sketchpad: Visual slave system in Baddeley's WM. Visual cache + inner scribe. Limited to ~3-4 items.
episodic buffer: Baddeley's 2000 addition. Multi-modal temporary store binding info from slave systems + LTM. Capacity ~4 chunks.
miller's magic number: G.A. Miller (1956). Short-term capacity ~ 7 ± 2 chunks. Foundational paper for cognitive psychology.
atkinson-shiffrin model: Multistore model (1968). Sensory → Short-Term Store → Long-Term Store via rehearsal. Classic; criticised for ignoring depth + parallel processes.
freud: Sigmund Freud (1856-1939). Psychoanalysis. Topographical (cs/pcs/ucs) + structural (id/ego/superego) models; psychosexual stages; defense mechanisms; dream analysis.
jung: Carl Jung (1875-1961). Analytical psychology. Collective unconscious, archetypes (anima/animus, shadow, self), individuation, introvert/extravert types.
adler: Alfred Adler (1870-1937). Individual psychology. Inferiority complex, striving for superiority, birth order, social interest (Gemeinschaftsgefühl).
erikson: Erik Erikson. Eight psychosocial stages, each a crisis (trust v mistrust → integrity v despair). Identity v role confusion in adolescence is best known.
horney: Karen Horney. Neo-Freudian. Basic anxiety, womb envy (vs Freud's penis envy), 3 neurotic trends: moving toward / against / away from people.
sullivan: Harry Stack Sullivan. Interpersonal psychiatry. Self-system, parataxic distortions. 'Personality is the relatively enduring pattern of recurrent interpersonal situations.'
fromm: Erich Fromm. Escape from Freedom; productive vs non-productive orientations; humanistic psychoanalysis.
klein: Melanie Klein. Object-relations pioneer. Paranoid-schizoid and depressive positions in infancy; play therapy.
winnicott: D.W. Winnicott. 'Good-enough mother,' transitional objects, true vs false self, holding environment.
mahler: Margaret Mahler. Separation-individuation in infancy: autistic → symbiotic → differentiation → practising → rapprochement → object constancy.
kohut: Heinz Kohut. Self psychology. Selfobjects (mirroring, idealising, twinship); empathic failure → narcissistic pathology.
kernberg: Otto Kernberg. Object-relations integration. Borderline personality organisation; splitting; transference-focused psychotherapy.
bowlby: John Bowlby. Attachment theory. Internal working models; secure base; protest-despair-detachment in maternal separation.
ainsworth: Mary Ainsworth. Strange Situation (1970). Classified infant attachment: secure (B), avoidant (A), resistant/ambivalent (C). Main & Solomon added disorganised (D).
strange situation: Ainsworth's 20-min procedure of separations + reunions assessing 12-18 mo attachment. Coding from reunion behaviour, not separation distress.
secure attachment: B. Infant uses caregiver as secure base; protests separation but is comforted on reunion. ~60-65% of low-risk samples.
avoidant attachment: A. Infant shows little distress on separation, avoids caregiver on reunion. Often paired with rejecting / dismissive parenting.
resistant attachment: C. Ambivalent. Infant clingy + difficult to soothe on reunion; angry-passive bursts. Inconsistent caregiving.
disorganised attachment: D. Contradictory behaviour on reunion (approach + freezing). Linked to frightening/frightened parenting + trauma. Strongest predictor of later psychopathology.
bronfenbrenner: Urie Bronfenbrenner. Ecological systems theory (later bioecological). Nested microsystem → mesosystem → exosystem → macrosystem → chronosystem.
microsystem: Bronfenbrenner. Immediate face-to-face settings: family, peers, school, neighbourhood. Bidirectional influence with the child.
mesosystem: Bronfenbrenner. Interconnections between microsystems (e.g. parent–teacher communication).
exosystem: Bronfenbrenner. Settings the child doesn't directly participate in but which still affect them (parent's workplace, mass media).
macrosystem: Bronfenbrenner. Overarching cultural blueprint: ideologies, laws, customs, SES, religion.
chronosystem: Bronfenbrenner. Time dimension — life transitions and sociohistorical change (war, pandemic, divorce).
piaget: Jean Piaget. Cognitive development in 4 stages: sensorimotor (0-2), preoperational (2-7), concrete operational (7-11), formal operational (11+). Schemata, assimilation, accommodation.
vygotsky: Lev Vygotsky. Sociocultural theory. Zone of Proximal Development, scaffolding, private speech, MKO (more knowledgeable other). Culture-first development.
zone of proximal development: Vygotsky. Gap between what a learner can do alone and what they can do with help. Where instruction has maximal effect.
scaffolding: Wood, Bruner, Ross. Adult support tuned to learner's current level, faded as competence grows. Operationalises ZPD.
kohlberg: Lawrence Kohlberg. Three levels (preconventional, conventional, postconventional), 6 stages of moral reasoning. Heinz dilemma. Critiqued by Gilligan for male bias.
harlow: Harry Harlow. Wire vs cloth surrogate-mother experiments in rhesus monkeys. Contact comfort > food in attachment. Maternal deprivation → social pathology.
lorenz: Konrad Lorenz. Imprinting in greylag geese — a critical-period attachment. Ethology Nobel 1973 (with Tinbergen + von Frisch).
imprinting: Lorenz. Rapid attachment learning during a critical period to the first moving object (usually mother). Irreversible.
maslow: Abraham Maslow. Humanistic. Hierarchy of needs: physiological → safety → love/belonging → esteem → self-actualisation (later transcendence). Studied healthy exemplars.
self-actualisation: Maslow. Realising one's full potential — top of the hierarchy. Characteristics: autonomy, peak experiences, accurate reality perception.
rogers: Carl Rogers. Person-centred therapy. Core conditions: unconditional positive regard, empathy, congruence. Real self vs ideal self; fully functioning person.
unconditional positive regard: Rogers. Therapist's nonjudgmental acceptance of the client regardless of behaviour. One of the three core conditions of effective therapy.
congruence: Rogers. Genuineness/authenticity of the therapist — outer behaviour matches inner experience. Core condition.
ellis: Albert Ellis. Rational Emotive Behavior Therapy (REBT). ABC model: Activating event → Belief → Consequence. Dispute irrational beliefs.
abc model: Ellis (REBT). It's not the event but our Belief about the Activating event that drives the Consequence (emotion/behaviour). Distinct from Skinner's ABC of behaviour.
beck: Aaron Beck. Cognitive Therapy for depression. Cognitive triad: negative views of self / world / future. Automatic thoughts; schema; cognitive distortions.
cognitive triad: Beck. Depressed thinking pattern: negative views of self, world, future. Targeted in CT.
seligman: Martin Seligman. Learned helplessness (1967, with Maier) → reformulated to attributional theory of depression. Later founded positive psychology.
learned helplessness: Seligman & Maier. Inescapable shock → animals stop trying even when escape becomes possible. Model of depression. Attributional reformulation 1978.
festinger: Leon Festinger. Cognitive dissonance theory (1957). Inconsistent cognitions create discomfort that motivates attitude change. $1/$20 boring-task study.
cognitive dissonance: Festinger. Tension from holding inconsistent cognitions; we reduce it by changing attitudes, adding consonant cognitions, or trivialising the inconsistency.
milgram: Stanley Milgram. Obedience studies (1961-63). ~65% of participants delivered max 'shock' under experimenter command. Ethical landmark.
asch: Solomon Asch. Conformity studies (1951). ~33% mean conformity to wrong unanimous majority on line-length task. Unanimity crucial; even one ally cuts it sharply.
zimbardo: Philip Zimbardo. Stanford Prison Experiment (1971). Role-induced abusive behaviour by 'guards'; aborted day 6. Methodological + ethical critiques are extensive.
bystander effect: Latané & Darley (after Kitty Genovese, 1964). Likelihood of helping decreases as the number of bystanders increases. Diffusion of responsibility.
diffusion of responsibility: Bystander effect mechanism: each bystander assumes someone else will act, so no one does. Latané & Darley.
social loafing: Reduced individual effort in groups (Ringelmann effect). Counteracted by identifiability + meaningful task + valued group.
groupthink: Janis. Cohesive groups pursue consensus over critical evaluation → poor decisions. Bay of Pigs, Challenger. Mitigation: devil's advocate, outside expertise.
group polarisation: Group discussion shifts decisions toward the average prior tendency, more extreme. Risky-shift in risk-prone groups; cautious-shift otherwise.
deindividuation: Loss of self-awareness in groups, especially anonymous ones. Lowered self-restraint → mob behaviour. Zimbardo, Diener.
social identity theory: Tajfel & Turner. We derive part of identity from group memberships → in-group favouritism, out-group derogation, even in minimal groups.
attribution theory: Heider, Kelley. How we explain behaviour: internal (dispositional) vs external (situational) causes. Kelley's covariation cube: consensus, consistency, distinctiveness.
fundamental attribution error: Ross. Overweighting dispositional causes + underweighting situational ones when explaining others' behaviour. Reduced in collectivist cultures.
actor-observer bias: Attribution: actors blame situation, observers blame disposition. Partly perspective + information differences.
self-serving bias: Attributing successes to oneself and failures to external factors. Protects self-esteem.
halo effect: One salient positive trait colours judgement of unrelated traits. Thorndike (1920) in officer ratings.
kelley's covariation model: Attribute behaviour to person/stimulus/situation based on consensus (do others act this way?), distinctiveness (does the actor act this way only here?), consistency (over time?).
elaboration likelihood model: Petty & Cacioppo. Two routes of persuasion: central (effortful processing of arguments) vs peripheral (cues: source, attractiveness, length).
weapon focus: Loftus. In threatening events, witnesses fixate on the weapon → poorer memory for the perpetrator's face.
loftus: Elizabeth Loftus. Misinformation effect; eyewitness memory plasticity. 'Smashed' vs 'hit' wording study (1974); Lost in the Mall.
misinformation effect: Loftus. Post-event information distorts memory of the original event. Demonstrated in countless eyewitness studies.
bartlett: Frederic Bartlett. War of the Ghosts (1932). Memory is reconstructive — fits new info to existing schemata; rationalisation, omission, transformation.
schema: Organised knowledge structure guiding perception and memory. Bartlett, Piaget. Speeds processing but distorts recall toward expectation.
tulving: Endel Tulving. Episodic / semantic distinction (1972); encoding-specificity principle; SPI model; PET subtraction studies of memory.
encoding specificity: Tulving. Memory is best when retrieval context matches encoding context. Underlies context- and state-dependent memory.
context-dependent memory: Recall better when external context at retrieval matches encoding (Godden & Baddeley underwater study).
state-dependent memory: Recall better when internal state (mood, drug) at retrieval matches encoding.
primacy effect: Better recall of early list items — encoded into LTM before rehearsal overload. Part of the serial-position curve.
recency effect: Better recall of last list items — still in STM at test. Eliminated by a 30-s distractor task.
serial position effect: U-shaped recall curve: primacy + recency. Murdock (1962). Evidence for separate STM and LTM stores.
levels of processing: Craik & Lockhart (1972). Deeper, semantic encoding produces better memory than shallow phonological/visual processing. Alternative to multi-store.
chunking: Grouping items into meaningful units to expand STM capacity. Miller. Expert advantage (chess masters) comes largely from richer chunks.
mnemonic: Memory aid. Method of loci, peg-word, acronyms, narrative. All exploit elaboration + organisation + dual-coding.
method of loci: Mnemonic: associate items with familiar locations along a mental route. Used by ancient orators; still effective.
dual coding: Paivio. Information encoded both verbally and visually is better remembered. Concrete > abstract words.
reliability: Consistency of measurement. Test-retest, inter-rater, internal consistency (α), parallel forms, split-half.
validity: Does the test measure what it claims? Content, criterion (concurrent + predictive), construct (convergent + discriminant). A test must be reliable to be valid, but reliability ≠ validity.
cronbach's alpha: Internal consistency reliability. Mean of all possible split-half correlations. ≥ 0.7 acceptable, ≥ 0.8 good, ≥ 0.9 excellent.
type i error: False positive — rejecting H0 when it's true. Probability = α (conventionally .05).
type ii error: False negative — failing to reject H0 when it's false. Probability = β; power = 1−β.
power: Probability of correctly rejecting a false H0. = 1 − β. Driven by effect size, sample size, α, design. Conventional target ≥ 0.80.
p value: Probability, assuming H0 is true, of obtaining a result as extreme as the one observed. p < .05 conventionally 'significant.' NOT P(H0 | data).
effect size: Standardised magnitude of difference / association independent of sample size. Cohen's d, r, η², odds ratio. Reported alongside p.
cohen's d: Effect size = (M1 − M2) / pooled SD. Cohen's benchmarks: 0.2 small, 0.5 medium, 0.8 large.
confidence interval: Range that would capture the true parameter in (1−α)% of repeated samples. 95% CI excluding 0 ≈ significant at p<.05.
regression to the mean: Extreme scores on the first measurement tend to move toward the mean on the second. Threat to single-group pre-post designs.
factor analysis: Data reduction: many observed variables → few latent factors. Exploratory (EFA) vs Confirmatory (CFA). Cattell, Eysenck, Big Five all built with FA.
big five: OCEAN: Openness, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, Neuroticism. McCrae & Costa NEO-PI-R. Replicable across cultures.
eysenck: Hans Eysenck. PEN model: Psychoticism, Extraversion, Neuroticism. Biological basis (cortical arousal in extraversion). Built EPI/EPQ.
cattell: Raymond Cattell. 16PF based on factor analysis. Surface vs source traits. Fluid (Gf) vs crystallised (Gc) intelligence.
allport: Gordon Allport. Trait theory. Cardinal, central, secondary traits. Functional autonomy; emphasised individual uniqueness.
mmpi: Minnesota Multiphasic Personality Inventory. 567 items (MMPI-2), 10 clinical scales + L/F/K validity scales. Criterion-keyed. Hathaway & McKinley 1943.
tat: Thematic Apperception Test. Murray & Morgan (1935). 31 ambiguous picture cards; subject tells a story. Projective; scored for needs + presses or psychodynamic themes.
rorschach: 10 symmetrical inkblots (1921). Exner Comprehensive System now widely replaced by R-PAS. Codes location, determinants, form quality, content, populars.
wais: Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale. Currently WAIS-IV: 15 subtests across 4 indices (VCI, PRI, WMI, PSI). Ages 16-90. Deviation IQ M=100, SD=15.
wisc: Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children. WISC-V (2014): 5 primary indices (VCI, VSI, FRI, WMI, PSI). Ages 6-16.
wppsi: Wechsler Preschool & Primary Scale of Intelligence. WPPSI-IV. Ages 2:6-7:7. Two batteries by age.
stanford-binet: American adaptation of the Binet-Simon scale. SB-5 (2003): 5 factors (KN, QR, VS, WM, FR), verbal + nonverbal. Ages 2-85+.
raven's progressive matrices: Non-verbal fluid-intelligence test. SPM (60 items), CPM (36), APM (36). Culture-fair-ish; high g-loading.
binet: Alfred Binet. With Théodore Simon, developed the first practical intelligence test (1905) to identify children needing extra schooling. Origin of mental age.
spearman: Charles Spearman. Two-factor theory of intelligence: g (general) + s (specific). Invented factor analysis + the rank correlation.
thurstone: Louis Thurstone. Seven Primary Mental Abilities (PMAs): V, W, S, P, N, R, M. Rejected pure g.
cattell-horn: Gf-Gc theory: Fluid intelligence (Gf — novel reasoning, peaks ~20s) + Crystallised (Gc — knowledge, rises through life). Foundation of CHC.
chc theory: Cattell-Horn-Carroll. Three-stratum hierarchical model: g (III), 10 broad abilities (II), ~70 narrow (I). Backbone of modern IQ tests.
gardner: Howard Gardner. Multiple Intelligences (1983): linguistic, logical-mathematical, spatial, musical, bodily-kinesthetic, interpersonal, intrapersonal, naturalist (+existential). Critiqued empirically.
sternberg: Robert Sternberg. Triarchic theory: analytical + creative + practical intelligence. Successful Intelligence model.
iq: Intelligence Quotient. Originally MA/CA × 100 (Stern, Terman). Now deviation IQ (Wechsler 1939): M=100, SD=15. ≥130 gifted; <70 + adaptive deficit = ID.
dsm-5: Diagnostic & Statistical Manual, 5th ed (APA 2013). Categorical psychiatric diagnosis. Sections II (categorical) + III (alternative dimensional models, esp. PD).
icd-11: International Classification of Diseases, 11th rev (WHO 2019, effective 2022). Includes mental disorders chapter. Dimensional PD section; complex PTSD; prolonged grief.
bipolar disorder: Recurrent mood episodes. BD-I = ≥1 manic episode; BD-II = hypomania + major depression; cyclothymia milder. Strongly heritable. First-line lithium.
obsessive-compulsive disorder: OCD. Recurrent intrusive obsessions + ritualistic compulsions to neutralise. Cortico-striato-thalamo-cortical circuit. ERP + SSRI first-line.
ptsd: Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder. Re-experiencing, avoidance, negative cognitions/mood, hyperarousal ≥1 month post-trauma. Hippocampal + amygdala dysregulation. Trauma-focused CBT, EMDR, SSRIs.
emdr: Eye Movement Desensitisation and Reprocessing. Shapiro 1987. 8-phase protocol with bilateral stimulation while recalling trauma. Effective for PTSD; mechanism debated.
borderline personality disorder: BPD. Affective instability, identity diffusion, impulsivity, self-harm, fear of abandonment, splitting. DBT (Linehan) first-line. High suicide risk.
splitting: Kernberg / Kleinian defense. Inability to integrate good and bad object representations → all-or-nothing views of self + others. Hallmark of BPD.
defense mechanism: Freud / A. Freud / Vaillant. Unconscious processes that distort reality to reduce anxiety. Mature (sublimation, humour) → neurotic (repression, intellectualisation) → immature (projection, splitting) → psychotic (denial of reality).
sublimation: Mature defense. Channels socially unacceptable impulses into acceptable activities (aggression → surgery, sport).
repression: Neurotic defense. Keeps threatening material out of consciousness. Distinguished from suppression (deliberate).
projection: Immature defense. Attributes one's own unacceptable thoughts to others ('it's not me, it's you').
displacement: Neurotic defense. Redirects an impulse from a threatening target to a safer one (yelled at by boss → yells at kid).
reaction formation: Neurotic defense. Converts an impulse into its opposite (homophobic crusader with same-sex attraction).
rationalisation: Neurotic defense. Constructs plausible but false reasons for behaviour ('the fox + sour grapes').
regression: Neurotic defense. Reverts to behaviours of an earlier developmental stage under stress.
denial: Psychotic defense. Refuses to accept external reality.
id: Freud. Unconscious reservoir of biological drives; pleasure principle. Present at birth.
ego: Freud. Mediator between id, superego, reality. Reality principle. Uses defense mechanisms.
superego: Freud. Internalised moral standards (conscience + ego ideal). Develops via parental introjection (~age 3-5).
pleasure principle: Freudian id principle: seek immediate gratification, avoid pain.
reality principle: Freudian ego principle: delay gratification to fit constraints of external reality.
psychosexual stages: Freud. Oral (0-1), Anal (1-3), Phallic (3-6), Latency (6-12), Genital (12+). Fixation at any stage shapes adult personality.
oedipus complex: Freud. Phallic-stage child's unconscious desire for opposite-sex parent + rivalry with same-sex. Resolved via identification + superego formation.
electra complex: Jung's term for the female version of the Oedipus complex. Freud rejected the parallel.
transference: Patient unconsciously redirects feelings from past relationships onto the therapist. Core psychoanalytic working tool.
countertransference: Therapist's emotional reactions to the patient, partly driven by therapist's own history. Now seen as informative, not just an obstacle.
free association: Freud's core technique. Patient says whatever comes to mind without censoring; resistances + slips reveal unconscious content.
dream analysis: Freud. Manifest content vs latent content. Condensation, displacement, symbolisation, secondary revision. 'Royal road to the unconscious.'
transactional analysis: Eric Berne. Parent / Adult / Child ego states; transactions; games people play; life scripts.
gestalt therapy: Fritz Perls. Awareness in the here-and-now; empty-chair technique; unfinished business; 'I am responsible for my experience.'
logotherapy: Viktor Frankl. Meaning-centred existential therapy. 'Man's Search for Meaning.' Paradoxical intention.
dialectical behaviour therapy: DBT, Linehan. CBT + mindfulness + acceptance. 4 modules: mindfulness, distress tolerance, emotion regulation, interpersonal effectiveness. First-line for BPD.
act: Acceptance and Commitment Therapy. Hayes. Six processes: defusion, acceptance, present moment, self-as-context, values, committed action. Third-wave CBT.
mindfulness: Non-judgmental moment-to-moment awareness. Core of MBSR (Kabat-Zinn), MBCT, DBT, ACT. Evidence base in depression relapse, chronic pain, anxiety.
behaviour modification: Systematic application of operant + classical learning to change behaviour. Token economies, contingency management, exposure.
systematic desensitisation: Wolpe. Reciprocal inhibition: pair relaxation with graded exposure to anxiety hierarchy. Classic phobia treatment.
exposure therapy: Repeated, prolonged contact with feared stimulus without avoidance → habituation + new safety learning. Imaginal, in vivo, virtual reality. Core for OCD, PTSD, phobias.
flooding: Maximum-intensity exposure all at once, no graded hierarchy. Faster but tougher than systematic desensitisation.
token economy: Behaviour therapy: target behaviours earn tokens redeemable for back-up reinforcers. Used in inpatient + classroom settings.
biofeedback: Real-time feedback of physiological signals (HR, EMG, GSR, EEG) so patient can learn voluntary control. Used for tension headache, hypertension, ADHD.
premack principle: High-probability behaviour reinforces low-probability behaviour ('eat your veggies, then play'). Used widely in applied behaviour analysis.
watson: John B. Watson. Behaviourism's manifesto (1913). Little Albert (1920) conditioned-fear study with Rosalie Rayner. Rejected introspection.
little albert: Watson & Rayner (1920). Conditioned an 11-month-old to fear a white rat by pairing it with a loud noise; generalisation to furry objects. Ethically appalling.
thorndike: Edward Thorndike. Puzzle-box cats; Law of Effect — behaviours followed by satisfying consequences are 'stamped in.' Precursor to Skinner.
law of effect: Thorndike. Responses producing satisfying outcomes are strengthened; punishing outcomes weaken responses. Forerunner of operant conditioning.
köhler: Wolfgang Köhler. Gestalt psychologist. Chimpanzee 'insight learning' on Tenerife (banana + boxes). Sudden reorganisation, not trial-and-error.
insight learning: Köhler. Sudden 'aha!' restructuring of a problem (chimps stacking boxes). Gestalt evidence against pure trial-and-error.
max wertheimer: Founder of Gestalt psychology (1912). Phi phenomenon (apparent motion). 'The whole is different from the sum of its parts.'
phi phenomenon: Wertheimer. Perception of motion from sequentially flashed stationary lights. Foundational Gestalt demo.
gestalt principles: Laws of perceptual organisation: proximity, similarity, continuity, closure, common fate, figure-ground. Wertheimer / Koffka / Köhler.
wundt: Wilhelm Wundt. Founded the first psychology lab (Leipzig 1879). Structuralism via introspection of conscious elements.
james: William James. Functionalism. 'Principles of Psychology' (1890). Stream of consciousness, pragmatism, James-Lange theory of emotion.
james-lange theory: Emotion follows from bodily response: 'we are afraid because we run.' Critiqued by Cannon-Bard.
cannon-bard theory: Emotion and bodily response occur simultaneously, both driven by thalamus → cortex + ANS. Counter to James-Lange.
schachter-singer: Two-factor theory of emotion (1962): physiological arousal + cognitive label → emotion. Epinephrine + confederate study.
lazarus: Richard Lazarus. Cognitive-appraisal theory of emotion. Primary appraisal (threat?) + secondary appraisal (cope?) precede emotion + physiology.
yerkes-dodson law: Inverted-U: performance peaks at moderate arousal. Optimal arousal lower for complex tasks, higher for simple ones.
type a personality: Friedman & Rosenman. Hostile, time-urgent, competitive. Linked to coronary heart disease — hostility component especially.
type d personality: Distressed personality (Denollet). Negative affectivity + social inhibition. Independent CHD risk factor.
general adaptation syndrome: Hans Selye. Alarm → Resistance → Exhaustion stages of stress response. Foundation of stress physiology.
selye: Hans Selye. Coined 'stress'; defined the General Adaptation Syndrome; eustress vs distress. Stress = nonspecific response.
hassles and uplifts: Lazarus & Folkman. Daily microstressors (hassles) and positive events (uplifts) better predict health than major life events.
social readjustment rating scale: Holmes & Rahe (1967). Life Change Units assigned to events (death of spouse = 100). Higher LCU → higher illness risk in next year.
festinger and carlsmith: 1959 cognitive-dissonance classic. $1 group rated boring task more enjoyable than $20 group — less external justification → bigger attitude change.
stanford prison experiment: Zimbardo 1971. College students role-played as guards / prisoners in mock prison. Aborted day 6. Now heavily critiqued methodologically + ethically.
kitty genovese: 1964 NYC murder reported (inaccurately) as ignored by 38 witnesses. Sparked Latané & Darley's bystander work.
darley and latané: Bystander effect research. Smoke-filled room, lady in distress, seizure studies. 5-step model: notice → interpret → assume responsibility → know how → act.
minority influence: Moscovici. A consistent committed minority can shift majority opinion via private informational influence (conversion) rather than public compliance.
moscovici: Serge Moscovici. Minority influence; consistent minorities produce private conversion. Blue-green slide experiment.
sherif: Muzafer Sherif. Robbers Cave (1954): in-group / out-group conflict + reduction via superordinate goals. Autokinetic-effect study of norm formation.
robbers cave: Sherif's 1954 boys'-camp field experiment. Demonstrated realistic conflict theory + reduction of prejudice through cooperative superordinate goals.
realistic conflict theory: Sherif. Intergroup hostility arises from competition over limited resources; reduced by cooperative interdependence.
contact hypothesis: Allport (1954). Intergroup contact reduces prejudice under conditions: equal status, common goals, cooperation, institutional support.
stereotype: Cognitive structure: beliefs about characteristics of a social group. Energy-efficient but distorting. Activated automatically; can be suppressed effortfully.
prejudice: Affective negative attitude toward a group. Distinct from stereotype (cognitive) and discrimination (behavioural).
discrimination: Behavioural enactment of prejudice / stereotypes — unfair treatment of a person because of group membership.
ingroup bias: Tendency to favour one's own group over outgroups. Emerges even in minimal-group settings (Tajfel).
outgroup homogeneity: Members of an outgroup are perceived as more similar to one another ('they all look alike') than members of the ingroup.
stereotype threat: Steele & Aronson. Awareness of negative stereotype about one's group impairs performance on a stereotype-relevant task.
self-fulfilling prophecy: Merton / Rosenthal. Expectations about a target change one's behaviour toward them, eliciting confirming behaviour. Pygmalion in the Classroom.
pygmalion effect: Rosenthal & Jacobson (1968). Teachers' raised expectations about randomly selected 'bloomer' students improved those students' actual IQ scores.
false consensus effect: Overestimating how widely others share one's opinions / behaviours.
false uniqueness effect: Underestimating how common one's desirable behaviours / abilities are.
availability heuristic: Tversky & Kahneman. Judging frequency by ease of retrieval → overestimating vivid / recent risks.
representativeness heuristic: Tversky & Kahneman. Judging probability by similarity to a prototype → base-rate neglect (Linda problem).
anchoring heuristic: Estimates assimilate to a starting anchor, even an irrelevant one. Robust + hard to debias.
framing effect: Choice depends on how options are described. Gains framing → risk-aversion; losses framing → risk-seeking. Asian Disease problem.
prospect theory: Kahneman & Tversky (1979). Decisions under risk: reference-dependent, loss aversion (~2×), diminishing sensitivity, probability weighting.
loss aversion: Losses loom larger than equivalent gains (~2:1). Core of prospect theory; explains endowment effect, status-quo bias.
endowment effect: We value owned items more than identical unowned ones. Driven by loss aversion (Thaler).
sunk cost fallacy: Continuing to invest because of irrecoverable past costs. Rational choice ignores sunk costs.
confirmation bias: Seeking + weighting evidence consistent with existing beliefs; under-weighting disconfirming info. Wason 2-4-6 task.
hindsight bias: 'Knew-it-all-along' effect. After learning an outcome, we overestimate the probability we'd have assigned beforehand.
dunning-kruger effect: Less competent individuals overestimate their competence; experts often underestimate theirs. Partly metacognitive failure.
self-efficacy: Bandura. Belief in one's capacity to perform actions needed to attain a goal. Predicts effort + persistence.
self-esteem: Global evaluative attitude toward the self. Rosenberg SES is the standard measure.
locus of control: Rotter. Internal (outcomes from own actions) vs external (outcomes from fate / chance / powerful others). Predicts coping, achievement.
rotter: Julian Rotter. Social-learning theory. Locus of control scale. Expectancy + reinforcement value.
mischel: Walter Mischel. Marshmallow test of delayed gratification. Person-situation debate + CAPS (Cognitive-Affective Personality System).
marshmallow test: Mischel's preschool delay-of-gratification task. Wait for two marshmallows or take one now. Predicted later academic + social outcomes (effect size now debated).
guilford: J.P. Guilford. Structure of Intellect (~120-180 cells). Divergent vs convergent thinking — foundation of creativity research.
divergent thinking: Generating many novel responses to an open prompt. Hallmark of creativity in Guilford / Torrance frameworks.
convergent thinking: Single-best-answer reasoning. Standard IQ tests measure this; less correlated with creativity.
torrance test: TTCT — most widely used creativity test. Verbal + figural subtests scored for fluency, flexibility, originality, elaboration.
creativity: Production of ideas / works that are both novel and useful (in context). Sternberg investment theory; Csikszentmihalyi systems model.
flow: Csikszentmihalyi. Optimal experience: complete absorption when challenge matches skill. Loss of self-consciousness, time distortion, intrinsic reward.
intrinsic motivation: Doing an activity for its own sake. Deci & Ryan. Tangible rewards can undermine it (overjustification effect).
extrinsic motivation: Doing an activity for separable rewards / pressures. Self-determination theory grades it by internalisation (external → introjected → identified → integrated).
self-determination theory: Deci & Ryan. Three basic psychological needs: autonomy, competence, relatedness. Their satisfaction supports intrinsic motivation + wellbeing.
achievement motivation: McClelland. nAch — drive to attain standards of excellence. Measured via TAT stories. High nAch favours moderate-difficulty tasks.
mcclelland: David McClelland. Need theory: nAch, nPow, nAff. TAT-based measurement. 'The Achievement Society.'
maslow hierarchy: Maslow. Pyramid: physiological → safety → love/belonging → esteem → self-actualisation (later self-transcendence). Lower needs are largely (not strictly) prepotent.
rogers fully functioning person: Rogers. Healthy outcome of person-centred development: open to experience, existential living, organismic trust, experiential freedom, creativity.
kelly: George Kelly. Personal Construct Theory. People are 'scientists' testing bipolar personal constructs (good-bad, warm-cold). Rep grid assessment.
personal construct: Kelly. Bipolar mental dimension a person uses to construe events. Repertory grid maps an individual's construct system.
rep grid: Kelly. Matrix in which elements (people, situations) are rated on the person's own constructs; reveals construct structure.
fechner: Gustav Fechner. Psychophysics founder (1860). Weber-Fechner law: perceived intensity ∝ log of stimulus intensity.
weber's law: ΔI / I = k (Weber fraction). Just-noticeable difference is a constant proportion of baseline intensity.
absolute threshold: Smallest detectable stimulus 50% of the time. Classical psychophysics.
difference threshold: Smallest detectable difference between two stimuli (JND). Weber's law describes how it scales.
signal detection theory: Replaces single threshold with sensitivity (d′) + bias (β / criterion). Separates perceptual ability from decision criterion.
place theory: Hearing. Pitch is coded by which part of the basilar membrane is maximally displaced (Helmholtz / Békésy). Explains high-frequency pitch.
frequency theory: Hearing. Pitch is coded by the rate of auditory-nerve firing matching sound frequency. Explains low frequencies; combines with place via volley theory.
volley theory: Wever. Groups of auditory neurons fire in volleys so the population firing rate can match higher frequencies than any single neuron. Mediates mid-range pitch.
békésy: Georg von Békésy. Travelling-wave demonstration on the basilar membrane → mechanical place coding of pitch. 1961 Nobel.
trichromatic theory: Young-Helmholtz. Three cone types (S/M/L) mix to give all colours. Confirmed by cone biology.
opponent process theory: Hering. Red-green, blue-yellow, black-white antagonistic channels. Explains afterimages + complementary colours. Operates downstream of cones.
cones: Photoreceptors for colour + acuity. ~6 million per retina; concentrated in fovea. Three types: S (blue), M (green), L (red).
rods: Photoreceptors for low-light vision. ~120 million per retina; peripheral; one pigment (rhodopsin); achromatic.
fovea: Central retinal pit of cones, 1:1 cone-to-ganglion ratio. Highest visual acuity. No rods.
saccade: Rapid ballistic eye movement between fixations. ~3 per second; suppression of visual processing during the flight.
feature integration theory: Treisman. Preattentive parallel feature maps (colour, orientation) → focal attention binds features into objects. Predicts illusory conjunctions.
attention: Selective allocation of limited cognitive resources. Bottom-up (salience) vs top-down (goal-driven). Networks: alerting, orienting, executive (Posner).
cocktail party effect: Cherry. Ability to focus on one conversation in a noisy room and still notice salient cues (own name) on the ignored channel.
broadbent: Donald Broadbent. Early-selection filter model of attention. Dichotic-listening evidence from cocktail-party studies.
deutsch-norman model: Late-selection attention model. All inputs processed for meaning; selection happens at response. Counterpart to Broadbent's early filter.
stroop effect: Naming the ink colour of a colour-word (RED in blue ink). Reading is automatic and interferes with colour naming. Measure of executive control.
automatic vs controlled: Shiffrin & Schneider. Automatic processes: fast, parallel, no awareness, no capacity load. Controlled: slow, serial, conscious, capacity-limited.
exner: John Exner. Comprehensive System for Rorschach scoring (1974), aiming to make it psychometric. Now largely succeeded by R-PAS.
linehan: Marsha Linehan. Developed DBT for chronic suicidality + BPD. Biosocial theory of emotional dysregulation.
yalom: Irvin Yalom. Existential + group psychotherapy. 11 therapeutic factors of group therapy; 4 existential givens (death, freedom, isolation, meaninglessness).
therapeutic factors: Yalom's 11 curative factors in group therapy: instillation of hope, universality, info, altruism, recapitulation of family, socialising techniques, imitative behaviour, interpersonal learning, group cohesiveness, catharsis, existential factors.
thomas and chess: NYLS (1956-onward). 9 temperament dimensions; 3 categories: easy, slow-to-warm-up, difficult. Goodness-of-fit construct.
temperament: Constitutional, biologically-rooted style of reactivity + self-regulation. Stable across infancy → adult personality.
vaillant: George Vaillant. Defense-mechanism maturity hierarchy (4 levels). Grant Study longitudinal data on adult development.
anna freud: Daughter of Sigmund. Systematised defense mechanisms (1936); pioneer of child psychoanalysis. Rival of Klein.
red nucleus: Midbrain motor relay. Receives M1 + cerebellar input; gives rise to the rubrospinal tract. Not part of the basal ganglia. Carries the dorsolateral corticorubrospinal pathway.
rubrospinal tract: Descending motor tract from the red nucleus. Crosses in midbrain, controls distal limb muscles in parallel to corticospinal.
association cortex: Higher-order cortex not directly sensory or motor. Integrates inputs and plans behaviour. Prefrontal (executive), posterior parietal (multisensory + action), temporal (object + language).
dorsolateral prefrontal cortex: DLPFC. Top of the motor planning hierarchy. Working memory, executive control, decision to initiate movement. Lesion → perseveration, abulia.
intrafusal motor neuron: Gamma motor neuron. Innervates intrafusal fibers inside muscle spindles; keeps spindle taut as muscle shortens.
isometric contraction: Muscle generates force without changing length (holding a weight steady). Length constant, tension rises.
dynamic contraction: Muscle changes length while producing force. Concentric (shortens, lifting) or eccentric (lengthens, lowering).
fast muscle fibers: Type II. Fast-twitch, fatigue quickly, anaerobic. Recruited late for high-force / sprint efforts. Innervated by large α-motor neurons.
slow muscle fibers: Type I. Slow-twitch, fatigue-resistant, oxidative. Recruited first (size principle). Postural and endurance work.
size principle: Henneman. Motor units are recruited from smallest to largest as force demand rises. Slow fibers first, fast last.
deep cerebellar nuclei: Four bilateral output nuclei of the cerebellum: fastigial, globose, emboliform, dentate (lateral → medial reverse). Receive Purkinje inhibition; send the cerebellar output.
dentate nucleus: Largest, most lateral deep cerebellar nucleus. Output of the cerebrocerebellum to thalamus → motor + prefrontal cortex. Damage → ataxia, dysmetria.
vestibular nucleus: Brainstem nucleus receiving vestibular (CN VIII) input. Drives vestibulo-ocular + vestibulo-spinal reflexes; relays to flocculonodular cerebellum.
motor neuron: Final common pathway for movement. α-motor neurons → extrafusal fibers (force); γ-motor neurons → intrafusal (spindle sensitivity). Cell body in ventral horn or brainstem motor nuclei.
michael graziano: Princeton neurophysiologist. Long-train (~500 ms) microstimulation of monkey precentral cortex evokes complex, goal-directed postures — argued for action-map (vs. homunculus) organisation of motor cortex.
miguel nicolelis: Duke neurophysiologist. With John Chapin, pioneered brain-machine interface in rats (1999) + monkeys ('Belle', 2002) — multielectrode arrays driving robotic effectors in real time.
brain-machine interface: BMI. Neural-activity recording (often multielectrode array) decoded in real time to control external effectors (cursor, prosthetic). Nicolelis/Chapin, Donoghue, Schwartz.
fadiga: Luciano Fadiga (Parma / Ferrara). Showed in humans via TMS that watching another's hand action increases corticospinal excitability of the matching muscle — mirror system in humans.
mirror system: Network responding both during action and observation in humans: ventral premotor (PMv), inferior parietal lobule, STS. Inferred from TMS, fMRI, EEG mu-suppression.
transcranial magnetic stimulation: TMS. Coil delivers magnetic pulse → focal cortical current. Single-pulse for mapping or 'virtual lesion'; rTMS for therapy (depression, OCD).
henneman: Elwood Henneman. Stated the size principle of motor-unit recruitment (1957): small α-motor neurons recruited before large ones as force demand rises.
motor unit: One α-motor neuron + all the extrafusal fibers it innervates. Small units (few fibers) for fine control (eye); large units (1000s) for power (gastrocnemius).
sherrington: Charles Sherrington. Coined 'synapse' (1897), 'proprioception,' 'final common path.' Reciprocal innervation of antagonist muscles. 1932 Nobel.
reciprocal innervation: Sherrington. When one muscle of an antagonist pair contracts, the antagonist relaxes via spinal interneuron inhibition. Allows smooth movement.
proprioception: Sense of body position + movement. Muscle spindles + Golgi tendon organs + joint receptors. Travels via the dorsal-column / medial-lemniscus pathway.
dorsal column medial lemniscus: Ascending pathway for fine touch + proprioception + vibration. Synapses in the medulla (gracile/cuneate nuclei), decussates, ascends as medial lemniscus to VPL of thalamus → S1.
spinothalamic tract: Ascending pathway for pain + temperature + crude touch. Synapses in spinal dorsal horn, decussates immediately, ascends contralaterally to VPL → S1.
cingulate motor areas: Motor regions in the medial wall cingulate (Brodmann 24/32). Project to spinal cord; integrate motor action with emotion + reward.
frontal lobe: Largest lobe. Motor (M1, premotor, SMA), prefrontal (executive, personality), Broca's. Damage → disinhibition, abulia, perseveration, apraxia.
parietal lobe: Somatosensory (S1) + posterior parietal association. Body schema, spatial attention, action planning. Right damage → contralateral neglect; left → apraxia, Gerstmann.
occipital lobe: Visual cortex (V1-V5). Damage → cortical blindness, achromatopsia, akinetopsia (V5/MT).
